cta.heading: Need a plant-specific recommendation?
cta.body: Share MW capacity, tilt or tracker layout, and soiling profile. Taypro engineers will map GLYDE, NYUMA, HELYX, or Opex to your asset.

hero.title: How Taypro compares to other solar panel cleaning robot companies in India
hero.lead: India's solar panel cleaning robot market is growing fast, with dozens of manufacturers competing for utility-scale O&M contracts. This page compares Taypro against Solabot Technologies, Aegeus Technologies, Skilancer Solar, and Vayu Solar on the criteria that matter to plant operators and EPC teams.

indianCompetitors.intro: All data below is sourced from each company's official website. Where a competitor does not publish a figure, it is marked as not disclosed.
indianCompetitors.sourceNote: Figures reflect publicly available information as of June 2026. Verify current specs on your RFQ before procurement decisions.
indianCompetitors.tableHeading: At a glance: Taypro vs competitors

0.taypro: Patented dual-pass: airflow first pass + self-cleaning microfiber drum

1.taypro: 4 Indian patents on dual-pass cleaning system and RF mesh communications

4.taypro: Yes: GLYDE-X (dual-pass) and NYUMA-X (PBT), purpose-built for single-axis trackers

7.taypro: NECTYR: schedules, block-wise cleaning logs, SCADA, AMC tickets, export reports

8.taypro: LTE, Wi-Fi, hybrid self-healing RF mesh, LoRa, LoRaWAN

9.taypro: Yes: OPEX: pay-per-panel-cleaned, Taypro operates the fleet
9.aegeus: Yes: MCaaS (Machine Cleaning as a Service)

11.taypro: Not published as a single fleet number (272 robots at Agar 200 MW alone)

13.taypro: TÜV NORD, NISE, UL, Karandikar Labs & Elca Labs: 30-year-equivalent module safety, IP65, static discharge, vibration, damp-heat & dry-heat (within safe limits)

14.taypro: LONGi, Solex, Premier Energies, RenewSys, Satvik, Trina Solar, Future Solar

15.taypro: Yes: pan-India, NECTYR remote diagnostics + field engineers

17.taypro: ORION plant intelligence platform (early access), CRADYL autonomous row-transfer docking

0.title: The only patented dual-pass cleaning system in India
0.body: Taypro holds 4 Indian patents covering the dual-pass mechanism, airflow first pass to dislodge loose particulate without surface contact, followed by a rotating self-cleaning microfiber drum for a complete wipe. The self-cleaning drum is a practical requirement at utility scale: a robot cleaning 3,600 modules per charge cannot deposit accumulated dust back onto cleaned panels. No other Indian manufacturer publishes an equivalent patented dual-pass system.

1.title: The only company with dedicated tracker robots across two cleaning technologies
1.body: Taypro offers tracker-compatible robots in both cleaning technologies, <glydeX>GLYDE-X</glydeX> for dual-pass microfiber and <nyumaX>NYUMA-X</nyumaX> for PBT: both purpose-engineered for single-axis tracker plants with flexible bodies and 360° bridge articulation. Solabot offers the SB-04T for trackers. Aegeus, Skilancer, and Vayu do not confirm tracker support on their websites.

2.title: The most comprehensive fleet software
2.body: NECTYR supports LTE, Wi-Fi, hybrid self-healing RF mesh, LoRa, and LoRaWAN: five connectivity options sized per site by Taypro engineers during commissioning. Competitors offer single-protocol solutions: Aegeus uses LoRaWAN, Solabot uses SCADA (protocol unspecified). NECTYR also includes block-wise scheduling, cleaning logs, AMC ticketing, and investor-grade report exports, features not published by any competitor.

3.title: Multi-laboratory hardware and module-safety certifications
3.body: Taypro robots are independently validated by TÜV NORD, the National Institute of Solar Energy (NISE), UL, Karandikar Labs, and Elca Labs. Accelerated testing simulates 30 years of daily cleaning cycles to confirm module safety, alongside IP65 rain and dust protection, static discharge tests, vibration tests for module stress from robot motion, and damp-heat and dry-heat conditioning for desert and coastal plants, all within safe limits. Aegeus holds UL India accreditation for its Unicorn robot. No equivalent certification stack is published by Solabot, Skilancer, or Vayu.

4.title: Named module OEM approvals
4.body: Taypro publicly names its approved module manufacturers: LONGi, Solex, Premier Energies, RenewSys, Satvik, Trina Solar, and Future Solar. Solabot claims 16 Tier 1 approvals but does not name them. Other competitors make general claims. Named approvals matter for warranty compliance on large IPP portfolios.

5.title: Largest single-deployment scale on record
5.body: Taypro's Agar 200 MW deployment of 272 robots is the largest publicly documented single-plant robotic cleaning fleet in India with verified data: 265 GLYDE automatic units, 7 semi-automatic, commissioned in 2024, generating an additional 7.50 GWh/year and saving 28 million litres of water annually. No competitor publishes equivalent plant-level data with robot counts and measured outcomes.

6.title: OPEX managed service with named commercial structure
6.body: Opex is a pay-per-panel-cleaned managed service where Taypro operates the fleet on the plant, with pricing transparency, defined cleaning cycles (3–10 per month based on soiling study), and full NECTYR reporting. Aegeus offers MCaaS as a service model. Solabot, Skilancer, and Vayu do not clearly publish an equivalent managed service on their websites.

howToRead.heading: How to read this comparison
howToRead.body: Robot count and project count measure market penetration. GW protected and cleaning technology depth measure operational capability at scale. For a 50–250 MW utility plant evaluating a robot partner, the questions that matter are: does the cleaning technology protect your module warranty, does the fleet software give you audit-ready O&M data, can the partner support same-day breakdown across India, and is there a commercial model that matches your balance sheet? On each of those criteria, Taypro publishes specific, verifiable answers, which is why this comparison draws only from official website sources.
howToRead.ctaLine: To evaluate Taypro's robots for your plant, <contact>request a quote</contact> or run a directional payback estimate on the <calculator>ROI calculator</calculator>. To compare individual robot models, see the <hub>full product hub</hub>.

0.q: Which Indian competitor has the most robots installed?
0.a: Solabot publishes 12,422+ robots installed on its homepage, the largest disclosed fleet count among Indian competitors. Taypro documents 5 GW+ deployed capacity and 272 robots at a single 200 MW plant.

1.q: How should IPPs evaluate vendors on this table?
1.a: Weight cleaning technology, tracker fit, fleet software audit capability, same-day breakdown support, and commercial model against your plant's MW scale and soiling profile, not headline robot or project counts alone.

2.q: Should we run a head-to-head trial?
2.a: Yes. Shortlist two vendors, run a 30–60 day soiling trial on representative blocks, and compare PR uplift, cycle time, logged coverage, and breakdown response before final procurement.

meta.title: Solar Panel Cleaning Robot vs Manual Cleaning in India
meta.description: Compare robotic vs manual solar panel cleaning for Indian utility plants: water use, labour safety, cycle frequency, fleet data, and when each model fits.

hero.title: Solar panel cleaning robot vs manual cleaning
hero.lead: Manual wet washing and robotic waterless cleaning both remove soiling, but they differ on water availability, labour risk, proof of completion, and long-run cost on MW-scale plants.

robotVsManual.intro: Utility IPPs in Rajasthan, Gujarat, and Madhya Pradesh often start with manual crews or tractor-mounted brushes, then evaluate robots when soiling losses, water cost, or safety incidents rise. This page compares decision criteria, not a single winner for every site.

0.manual: Typically requires water; scarce on arid sites

1.robot: Fewer rooftop/row walks; scheduled autonomous cycles
1.manual: Higher crew hours; heat and height exposure

2.manual: Often photo/checklist based; harder to audit at scale

3.robot: Higher frequency feasible without proportional labour
3.manual: Cost and crew limits reduce cadence

4.robot: CAPEX robots or Opex per-panel service

5.manual: Small C&I, abundant water, very low soiling

0.q: When does a robot beat manual cleaning?
0.a: When soiling losses exceed labour+water cost, water is limited, or owners need logged cleaning coverage for lenders and O&M contracts. Run Taypro's ROI calculator with your PR data.

1.q: Can robots and manual cleaning coexist?
1.a: Yes. Many plants use robots on bulk arrays and manual methods on switchyards, substations, or damaged tables until robots are deployed fleet-wide.

2.q: What does manual cleaning cost at 100 MW scale?
2.a: Manual economics scale linearly: more blocks mean more crews, tankers, and supervision. Robotic OPEX flattens marginal cost per additional cycle because one fleet covers repeatable rows with remote NECTYR oversight.

3.q: How do safety and insurance teams view manual vs robotic cleaning?
3.a: Manual row walking in heat and dust exposes crews to height and heat-stress risk. Autonomous robots reduce person-hours on tables, a factor in EHS reviews on SECI and state discom tenders.

deepDive.heading: MW-scale cost and audit comparison
deepDive.p1: Manual programmes hide cost in variable labour and water, easy to under-budget in year one, expensive when dust season demands weekly washes. Robots front-load CAPEX or convert to OPEX per panel cleaned, with block-level logs exportable for PR recovery analysis.
deepDive.p2: Asset managers under performance guarantees need evidence that cleaning happened on schedule. NECTYR timestamps routes, exceptions, and battery swaps; manual programmes often rely on photos and sign-off sheets that do not scale across 200+ blocks.
deepDive.p3: Break-even depends on soiling loss, tariff, and cycle frequency, not robot brochure price alone. Model your plant on the solar panel cleaning ROI calculator, then validate with a Taypro layout review.

meta.title: Waterless vs Water-Based Solar Panel Cleaning
meta.description: Compare waterless robotic and water-based manual solar cleaning: dust removal, module risk, water cost, environmental compliance, and Indian utility fit.

hero.title: Waterless vs water-based solar panel cleaning
hero.lead: Water washing remains common on smaller sites; waterless robots dominate new Indian utility RFQs where water is scarce and audit cadence is high.

waterlessVsWater.intro: Dust chemistry, module coating, and local water cost determine the right method. Taypro standardises on waterless dual-pass and PBT dry cleaning for utility arrays; water-based methods may still suit specific zones with reliable supply and low TDS.

0.waterless: None on-row; major savings in arid states
0.water: Litres per wash × table count × cycles

3.waterless: No runoff; aligns with water-scarce site permits

5.water: Low soiling C&I with cheap water access

0.a: Taypro uses dry microfiber and PBT methods designed for utility glass, validate on a pilot block with your module OEM guidance.

1.q: Can we switch from wet to waterless mid-life?
1.a: Yes. Retrofit robots on live plants; Opex can bridge until CAPEX approval.

2.q: How much water does manual washing use on utility plants?
2.a: Industry estimates often cite 140,000+ litres per MW per year for wet-wash programmes on large Indian sites, before tanker logistics and discharge compliance. Waterless robots eliminate on-row consumption entirely.

3.q: What do Indian environmental norms favour?
3.a: Water-scarce districts and CPSU tenders increasingly restrict groundwater draw for module washing. Dry robotic cadence aligns with permit constraints while enabling 3–10 cycles per month versus episodic wet washes.

deepDive.heading: TCO snapshot: waterless robots vs wet manual crews (utility scale)
deepDive.p1: On a 100 MW fixed-tilt plant in Rajasthan or Gujarat, manual wet washing typically bundles labour gangs, tanker sourcing, fuel, supervision, and irregular cadence. Robotic waterless OPEX converts cleaning to a per-panel variable cost with NECTYR proof of which blocks were cleaned each night, critical for IPP asset managers and lender audits.
deepDive.p2: Wet washing can still win on small C&I rooftops with municipal water access and low dust load. Taypro's utility offering is waterless GLYDE, NYUMA, and tracker variants, not pressure-wash services. Use the ROI calculator with your tariff, soiling rate, and cycle assumptions before locking a method in your O&M contract.
deepDive.p3: Regulatory pressure on groundwater use in solar parks (Bhuj, Jaisalmer, Phalodi belts) is pushing new RFQs toward dry methods even when legacy contracts assumed wet crews. Factor permit risk and water price escalation, not just year-one labour rates.

meta.title: GLYDE vs NYUMA: Fixed-Tilt Automatic Solar Cleaning Robots
meta.description: Compare Taypro GLYDE dual-pass microfiber and NYUMA single-pass PBT automatic robots: cleaning technology, soiling fit, consumables, range, deployments, and NECTYR fleet monitoring.

hero.title: GLYDE vs NYUMA: Which automatic robot fits your plant?
hero.lead: Both robots are fully autonomous, waterless, and managed from NECTYR. GLYDE is Taypro's patented dual-pass microfiber flagship; NYUMA is the PBT single-pass platform for capex-efficient fixed-tilt fleets.

glydeVsNyuma.intro: Fixed-tilt utility owners often shortlist both robots on the same RFQ. GLYDE prioritises maximum dust lift per cycle on mixed soiling; NYUMA wins when PBT brush economics and simpler consumables drive procurement.
glydeVsNyuma.tableHeading: GLYDE vs NYUMA at a glance

0.glyde: Patented dual-pass: airflow loosens dust, microfiber completes the wipe
0.nyuma: Single-pass counter-rotating PBT brush drum, fewer moving consumables

1.glyde: Flagship fixed-tilt rows where maximum dust lift per cycle is the priority
1.nyuma: Repeatable fixed-tilt blocks where PBT capex and brush economics win RFQs

2.glyde: Dual-pass excels on mixed dry and adhesive dust layers
2.nyuma: Ideal for dry desert dust; schedule extra cycles if cementitious soiling is heavy

3.glyde: Self-cleaning microfiber drum with extended service intervals
3.nyuma: UV-stable PBT brush with field-replaceable bristle cartridges

5.nyuma: Akhadana-scale and mixed-fleet PBT automatic programmes

7.glyde: NECTYR via LTE, Wi-Fi, RF mesh, LoRa, or LoRaWAN
7.nyuma: NECTYR via LTE, Wi-Fi, RF mesh, LoRa, or LoRaWAN

0.q: Can GLYDE and NYUMA run in the same plant?
0.a: Yes. Mixed fleets are common, GLYDE on high-soiling blocks, NYUMA on PBT-specified rows. NECTYR schedules both from one portal.

1.q: Which robot is better for Rajasthan dust?
1.a: Both handle dry desert dust. Choose GLYDE when adhesive or mixed layers need dual-pass lift; choose NYUMA when PBT capex and brush service economics dominate the RFQ.

2.q: How do procurement teams decide on the same RFQ?
2.a: Split blocks by soiling class: dual-pass GLYDE on adhesive-prone tables, NYUMA on dry high-throughput rows. NECTYR schedules both models from one portal with unified reporting.

deepDive.heading: Decision tree: GLYDE vs NYUMA on fixed-tilt utility plants
deepDive.p1: Start with soiling chemistry: dry desert dust favours either robot; cementitious or pollen-heavy layers tilt toward GLYDE dual-pass. Next, compare capex per MW and brush versus microfiber service intervals in your O&M budget.
deepDive.p2: If your EPC specified PBT in the tender, NYUMA is the natural fit. If the tender asks for maximum dust removal per cycle or dual-pass patents, GLYDE is the flagship. Both robots share NECTYR, pan-India service, and waterless operation, the split is technology economics, not software capability.
deepDive.p3: Validate robot count per MW with Taypro layout engineers before finalising, row length, gap width, and block count drive fleet size more than brand choice between these two platforms.

meta.title: GLYDE-X vs NYUMA-X: Single-Axis Tracker Cleaning Robots
meta.description: Compare Taypro GLYDE-X dual-pass and NYUMA-X single-pass PBT tracker robots: cleaning mechanism, NEXTracker compatibility, inter-table flex, weight, range, and NECTYR scheduling.

hero.title: GLYDE-X vs NYUMA-X: Which tracker robot should you specify?
hero.lead: Both robots traverse NEXTracker, Gamechanger, and equivalent single-axis tables with ±15° inter-table flex. GLYDE-X uses patented dual-pass microfiber; NYUMA-X uses single-pass PBT for capex-sensitive tracker programmes.

glydeXVsNyumaX.intro: Tracker O&M teams specify GLYDE-X when premium dust lift per night cycle matters; NYUMA-X fits large tracker fleets prioritising PBT capex and brush service economics. Both share NECTYR fleet telemetry.
glydeXVsNyumaX.tableHeading: GLYDE-X vs NYUMA-X at a glance

0.glydeX: Dual-pass microfiber, airflow plus wipe for mixed soiling
0.nyumaX: Single-pass PBT brush, optimised for dry tracker dust belts

2.glydeX: ±15° body articulation with validated bridge kits
2.nyumaX: ±15° body articulation with validated bridge kits

5.glydeX: Premium tracker rows needing maximum dust lift per night cycle
5.nyumaX: Large tracker fleets prioritising PBT capex and brush service economics

6.glydeX: Fully autonomous, AI weather-aware scheduling via NECTYR
6.nyumaX: Fully autonomous, AI weather-aware scheduling via NECTYR

7.glydeX: See GLYDE for fixed-tilt dual-pass cleaning
7.nyumaX: See NYUMA for fixed-tilt PBT cleaning

0.q: Do GLYDE-X and NYUMA-X need arrays parked flat?
0.a: No. Both clean from -52° to +52° module tilt and run after production hours with tracker-aware routing in NECTYR.

1.q: Which tracker robot matches fixed-tilt GLYDE or NYUMA?
1.a: GLYDE-X extends dual-pass microfiber to trackers; NYUMA-X extends the PBT single-pass platform. Compare fixed-tilt options on our GLYDE vs NYUMA guide.

2.q: Does CRADYL row-transfer affect the choice?
2.a: On wide row gaps, CRADYL docking pairs with either tracker robot, specify transfer points during layout review so fleet count includes docking stations, not just table robots.

deepDive.heading: Tracker RFQ decision guide
deepDive.p1: Single-axis plants add tilt-angle routing, inter-table flex, and night-window scheduling constraints. GLYDE-X targets premium dust lift where adhesive layers follow dust storms; NYUMA-X fits capex-sensitive tracker portfolios that already standardised on PBT for fixed-tilt blocks.
deepDive.p2: Both robots publish NEXTracker and Gamechanger compatibility with ±15° inter-table flex. Validate on your stow angles and row pitch, tracker geometry varies more than fixed-tilt repeatability. NECTYR schedules both models with weather hold logic shared across the Taypro fleet.
deepDive.p3: Mixed fleets are common: GLYDE-X on high-soiling tracker blocks, NYUMA-X on PBT-specified tables, fixed-tilt GLYDE or NYUMA on non-tracker sections. One NECTYR portal covers the combined fleet for asset-manager reporting.
